mnemonics.title: SCALE-IT Framework
mnemonics.content: S - Scalability planning, C - Capacity metrics, A - API traffic management, L - Load balancing, E - Elasticity implementation, I - Input/output optimization, T - Throughput monitoring
Viral content creates TRAFFIC spikes: T-rending topics, R-apid sharing, A-utomatic replication, F-eed updates, F-ollower explosions, I-nfluence amplification, C-ascading requests, S-torage surge
mnemonics.title: TRAFFIC Spikes Memory Aid

mnemonics.content: Capacity Planning needs CLOUDS: C-urrent metrics, L-oad prediction, O-ptimize resources, U-se caching, D-istribute with CDN, S-calability assessment
mnemonics.title: CLOUDS for Capacity

acronyms.usage: Allows third-party applications to interact with social platforms
Geographically distributes content to reduce latency
Content created by users like posts, images, videos
Defines acceptable performance metrics and uptime guarantees
Manages traffic priority to ensure consistent performance
Key metric for measuring API and website traffic load

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a mountain peak appearing suddenly - that's a viral content spike. Draw a flat baseline (normal traffic) with sudden sharp peaks (viral events) of varying heights.
Vertical = adding floors to a single building (more power, same location). Horizontal = building more houses in a neighborhood (more machines, distributed).
Draw a central hub (origin server) with multiple smaller nodes around it. Show user requests going to nearest node instead of traveling to central hub.
Draw an inverted pyramid: User → CDN Cache → Application Cache → Database. Each layer closer to user is faster but stores less data.
Imagine a toll gate with a counter. Each request increments counter. When limit reached, gate closes until reset time.
Draw users at bottom, load balancer in middle (funnel shape), servers at top. Show requests flowing through balancer to distribute evenly across servers.

analogies.analogy: Like a restaurant - normal dinner service is predictable, but a viral food review (influencer post) can suddenly fill the entire restaurant in minutes.
Like a theater ticket system - each client gets a limited number of tickets per show to prevent selling more than seats available.
Like having local library branches - instead of everyone going to the central library, you access books from the nearest branch.
Like adding more checkout counters during holiday shopping - each counter handles part of the crowd rather than making one counter faster.
Like a notepad near your phone charger - you write down frequently called numbers instead of looking them up every time.
Like a rubber band - expands to accommodate more load and contracts when demand decreases, saving resources.
